episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Science, Wonder & the Natural World
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Science, Wonder & the Natural World.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [ScienceDaily] Quantum ghost imaging works using only sunlight in stunning new experiment and [ScienceDaily] Scientists found a hidden Alzheimer’s trigger and shut it down, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How can biodiversity monitoring infrastructure be built in the Cariboo to detect ecological changes while protecting Indigenous data sovereignty and ensuring findings actually influence resource decisions? Topics include: Data sovereignty and Indigenous knowledge governance in monitoring systems, iNaturalist and citizen science as infrastructure for biodiversity observation, River dissolved oxygen decline and salmon habitat degradation in the Cariboo, Epistemic authority: what counts as evidence in resource management decisions, Multi-stakeholder convening as a pathway to integrated monitoring and decision-making.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: 100 Mile House Food Bank Society

articles.title: [ScienceAlert] 80% of Earth's Rivers Are Quickly Losing Oxygen, Study Reveals
articles.author: Jess Cockerill

discussion.central_question: How can biodiversity monitoring infrastructure be built in the Cariboo to detect ecological changes while protecting Indigenous data sovereignty and ensuring findings actually influence resource decisions?
discussion.riley_position: Citizen science platforms like iNaturalist can efficiently capture biodiversity data from people already working the land, and practical solutions exist (dissolved oxygen sensors, multi-stakeholder tables) if communities decide to deploy them.
riley_key_evidence: Ptilotus senarius rediscovered in Australia through iNaturalist after 60 years of presumed extinction
Dissolved oxygen sensors are affordable (few thousand dollars) and solvable instrumentation problems
Community Futures Cariboo-Chilcotin has precedent for convening neutral multi-stakeholder tables on resource questions
discussion.casey_position: Data collection without clear governance architecture and control by affected communities risks serving external institutions rather than protecting territories, and attention to ecological wonder must be paired with structural justice to justify monitoring.
casey_key_evidence: Parakneria thysi waterfall-climbing behavior was unknown to science but existed in local community knowledge, which was systematically excluded from institutional resource decisions
DFO stock assessments privilege certain data types and exclude Tŝilhqot'in oral traditions despite Supreme Court title recognition
Horsefly River sockeye crashes are unexplained partly because continuous oxygen data doesn't exist, yet sensor deployment requires prior governance agreement
discussion.resolution: They converged on agreement that both wonder (what motivates looking) and governance structure (what makes looking matter) must develop simultaneously, and that the practical starting point is a single multi-stakeholder conversation between ranchers, Indigenous nations, and DFO to identify what each party knows about watershed conditions before any new infrastructure.
topics_covered: Data sovereignty and Indigenous knowledge governance in monitoring systems
iNaturalist and citizen science as infrastructure for biodiversity observation
River dissolved oxygen decline and salmon habitat degradation in the Cariboo
Epistemic authority: what counts as evidence in resource management decisions
Multi-stakeholder convening as a pathway to integrated monitoring and decision-making
